quote: That that the the Tesla panels, they that's something only Tesla does. We're a certified Tesla arm installer, but only for their batteries. So that would be something that you would have to look into on. I know that there is, like, a two year back, wait period currently, I believe, with the with the at least their equipment.
stewart_read: Peter opened with 'I saw this thing about the Tesla panels' — a direct expectation gap trigger. Ty's reframe is partially correct (Ion is a certified installer, batteries only) but immediately drifts into speculative product education about Tesla's two-year wait period. The codex is explicit: only reframe when the customer raises it, then move to qualify and schedule. Ty reframed correctly but then spent 90 seconds in Tesla product commentary that served no setter purpose and consumed the window where Verify should have started.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Ty was building rapport by engaging Peter's stated interest before pivoting to qualification. Rejected — there is no subsequent pivot to qualification. The Tesla commentary ends with a shade DQ, not a Verify sequence.
coaching_implication: Show Ty the codex framing: acknowledge the referral, reframe in one sentence, then move immediately to 'let me ask you a couple of quick questions about the home.' The Tesla education was a scope drift that consumed the call's productive window.

quote: I talked to somebody about a week or two ago about solar, and it was, like, they wanted, like, 12 panels or something. So I thought this was a different deal.
stewart_read: Peter volunteers a direct hit on verify.prior_designs — a required Verify item. He has already had a solar conversation within the past two weeks, received a 12-panel design, and is now calling Ion because he thought it might be a different product. Ty never follows up on this. Who was the prior company? What did Peter think of the design? Was he close to signing? This is competitive context that would have sharpened the pitch and surfaced whether Peter was a comparison shopper or a motivated buyer looking for a reason to move. The 'they didn't wanna hear that' at 01:47 retroactively confirms he was motivated — he had a workaround idea and the prior company dismissed it.
alternative_hypothesis: Ty may have deprioritized the prior-designs follow-up because the shade DQ was already forming in his mind from the satellite view. Rejected — the shade DQ comes at 01:39, a full 38 seconds after Peter's prior-company disclosure. There was time to probe.
coaching_implication: Drill verify.prior_designs as a required capture. When a customer volunteers a prior company interaction this early, the correct move is a single follow-up question: 'What did you think of what they showed you?' That answer shapes the entire rest of the call.

quote: I I'm taking a look, and it looks like you have too much shade for us to make our system work anyway. So I'll just get back to us for now.
stewart_read: Ty issues a hard DQ on shade from satellite imagery before completing a single required Verify item. No bill, no interest depth, no title check, no prior designs follow-up, no roof age — the call is closed on a remote assessment alone. The shade reading may be accurate, but the DQ is premature: Peter has already signaled a workaround idea (ground racks) and a prior company interaction. A complete Verify sequence would have surfaced whether the shade issue was actually disqualifying or whether there was a routing path.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Ty identified a hard DQ condition early and chose to exit cleanly rather than waste both parties' time on a lead that can't convert. Rejected — Peter's subsequent workaround proposal (01:47) demonstrates the DQ was not as clean as Ty assumed, and the codex requires Verify completion before DQ issuance.
coaching_implication: Establish the rule: satellite shade assessments are a flag, not a DQ trigger. The correct move is to note the shade concern, continue Verify, and let the customer's own information (workaround ideas, roof plans, bill pain) inform whether there is a routing path before closing the call.

quote: Well, you know, I've said before, if they build racks in the back part of my yard, that would solve the problem. You know? But, they didn't wanna hear that.
stewart_read: Peter is not a passive lead — he has already proposed a concrete technical workaround to a prior company and been dismissed. 'They didn't wanna hear that' is a motivated buyer signal: he has thought about this, he has a solution in mind, and he is testing whether Ion will engage differently. This is also a second touch on the prior_designs Verify item — the prior company dismissed his ground-rack idea, which is why he is calling Ion. Ty's response ('my company, we also only do roof mounted systems') confirms the same dismissal pattern and closes the door on any differentiation.
alternative_hypothesis: Ty's response was accurate — Ion does not do ground-mount systems, so engaging the workaround would have been misleading. Partially valid, but the correct move is to probe the shade assessment first ('let me take a closer look at the specific shaded area') before confirming the limitation, and to acknowledge Peter's idea before closing.
coaching_implication: When a customer says 'they didn't wanna hear that,' they are handing the rep a differentiation opportunity. The coaching move: show Ty how to acknowledge the idea ('That's actually a smart workaround — let me take a closer look at the specific area before I say it won't work') before confirming any limitation. Even if the outcome is the same DQ, Peter leaves feeling heard rather than dismissed twice.

quote: Yeah. Unfortunately, my company, we also only do roof mounted systems, so I apologize about that as well.
stewart_read: This is the second consecutive apology-closure in 23 seconds. Stacking two 'unfortunately / I apologize' closures signals to the customer that Ion is the same as every other company that dismissed him. The word 'also' is particularly damaging — it explicitly aligns Ion with the prior company that 'didn't wanna hear' his workaround. There was no attempt between the two closures to probe whether the shade assessment was accurate, whether the ground-rack idea had any merit, or whether there was any routing path (e.g., a specialist who could assess the specific shaded area).
alternative_hypothesis: Ty was being honest and efficient — if Ion genuinely cannot serve this home, a clean exit is better than a false booking. Partially valid, but the double-apology framing and the 'also' alignment with the prior company's dismissal are avoidable regardless of the outcome.
coaching_implication: Coach the language of the exit, not just the decision to exit. 'I apologize' twice in 23 seconds is a confidence signal that works against Ion's legitimacy framing. A suggested rephrase intent: acknowledge Peter's workaround idea, confirm the limitation once, and leave a door open ('If your situation changes or you end up with a different roof configuration, we'd love to take another look').

quote: Not usually. I mean, it's it's their their main thing is the shingles right now. That's what they're pushing for is the shingles.
stewart_read: Ty is now two minutes into Tesla product education — explaining shingle coverage, power output, and roof replacement mechanics — on a call that has already been DQ'd. This is pure scope drift: the call has no appointment value, and the Tesla product information Ty is providing is speculative ('I believe,' 'Not usually,' 'might only power your microwave or something'). The codex is explicit that Ion does not sell Tesla solar panels or roof tiles, and that setter scope does not include product specification for competitor products.
alternative_hypothesis: Ty was maintaining rapport with a customer who was still engaged, keeping the door open for a future referral or re-engagement. Plausible, but the speculative framing ('I believe,' 'might only') undermines Ion's credibility rather than building it. If the goal was rapport maintenance, a cleaner move would be to acknowledge Peter's curiosity and redirect to Ion's actual value proposition.
coaching_implication: Flag the speculative product commentary on a competitor's product as a credibility risk. The coaching move: when a DQ'd customer asks about a competitor's product, the correct response is a brief honest acknowledgment followed by a clean close — not a two-minute product briefing with hedged language. Suggested rephrase intent: 'Tesla's shingle product is their own thing — I'd point you to their website for the specifics. For what we do at Ion, if your situation ever changes, we'd love to take another look.'

quote: Well, I was just gonna say right now that I have the horse in front of the carrot.
stewart_read: Peter's idiom signals that his curiosity is now satisfied — he came in with a misconception about Tesla's product, he has been corrected, and he is ready to close. This is a soft exit signal, not a vulnerability disclosure. Ty's 'Exactly. Yeah.' is register-appropriate — matching Peter's wry, self-aware tone. The minor missed opportunity is that Ty could have used this moment as a re-entry point ('Before I let you go — if your roof situation ever changes, I'd love to take another look at the home') but the window was narrow and Peter was clearly winding down.
alternative_hypothesis: Ty's 'Exactly. Yeah.' was a flat miss — he should have re-engaged with a curiosity hook or a future-path offer. Rejected — Peter's tone and subsequent 'Great. That's all' confirm he was satisfied and closing, not waiting for a re-engagement. A hard pivot here would have felt forced.
coaching_implication: Minor: coach Ty on a lightweight re-entry line for satisfied-exit moments — something that leaves a door open without fighting the customer's closure. This is a low-priority note; the call was already DQ'd and Peter's exit was clean.

quote: And, what got you interested in looking at solar?
stewart_read: Ty asks the interest question correctly and Peter gives a substantive answer (Tesla panels, prior company interaction, 12-panel design). This is the one Verify item Ty completes before the DQ. However, the answer Peter gives is product-curiosity driven, not bill-pain driven — and Ty never follows up with the bill anchor. The codex is explicit: interest_in_solar is 'THE EMOTIONAL ANCHOR' and top setters spend 60+ seconds here. Ty spends approximately 8 seconds on Peter's answer before pivoting to Tesla product education. The bill is never mentioned.
alternative_hypothesis: Ty may have deprioritized the bill anchor because the shade DQ was forming — no point anchoring bill pain on a lead that can't convert. Rejected — the shade DQ comes 52 seconds after the interest question, and the bill anchor would have informed whether the shade issue was worth probing further.
coaching_implication: Even on a call that trends toward DQ, completing the bill anchor serves two purposes: it confirms whether the lead has real pain worth routing around the DQ, and it gives the rep a stronger close if the DQ turns out to be incorrect. Drill the sequence: interest question → bill follow-up → then assess DQ conditions.
